coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of high-performance luminescent solar concentrators using N-doped carbon dots/PMMA mixed matrix slab
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiao
Ma, Wenwen
Li, Yunxia
Zhong, Lingqi
Li, Wenjing
Zhao, Xiujian
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) as alternatives to solar cells are suitable for photovoltaic windows. Generally semiconductor quantum dots are employed as emitters for LSC fabrication due to their advantages such as photostability, spectral tunability, and high photoluminescence quantum yield. In this paper, we used low cost and environmentally friendly N-doped carbon-dots (N-CDs) as phosphors to fabricate N-CDs/PMMA slab based luminescent solar concentrators. The high-performance N-CDs/PMMA slab based LSCs with an external optical efficiency of 12.23% could be achieved when the concentration of N-CDs within the PMMA matrix is 0.08 wt%. Our results showed that N-CDs/PMMA slab based luminescent solar concentrators make the practical use of LSCs more feasible because of the unique properties of N-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
External optical efficiency
Luminescent solar concentrators
High-performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the mixtures transferred into a casting mold.
The photograph of the labeled N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs after post-processing.

Optical properties of N-CDs. Absorption and normalized PL spectra of N-CDs in ethanol solutions (excitation wavelength at 360 nm). Inset: Photograph of N-CDs in ethanol solutions under natural light and 365 nm UV illumination.
(a) Absorption spectra and (b) transmission spectra of N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs with different concentrations of N-CDs.
Photoluminescence spectra of N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs with different concentrations of N-CDs at different excitation wavelength (in 10 nm increment starting from 340 nm to 530 nm).
Photoluminescence spectra of N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs with different concentrations of N-CDs under 370 nm (a) and 470 nm (b) excitation.
Photograph of pure polymer slab (A0) and N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slab (A3) under UV excitation in dark (λ = 365 nm).
Relation of photocurrent density and photovoltage (J–V) of the LSCs with different concentrations of N-CDs.
Photovoltaic parameters of the LSCs with different concentrations of N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of high-performance luminescent solar concentrators using N-doped carbon dots/PMMA mixed matrix slab

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) as alternatives to solar cells are suitable for photovoltaic windows. Generally semiconductor quantum dots are employed as emitters for LSC fabrication due to their advantages such as photostability, spectral tunability, and high photoluminescence quantum yield. In this paper, we used low cost and environmentally friendly N-doped carbon-dots (N-CDs) as phosphors to fabricate N-CDs/PMMA slab based luminescent solar concentrators. The high-performance N-CDs/PMMA slab based LSCs with an external optical efficiency of 12.23% could be achieved when the concentration of N-CDs within the PMMA matrix is 0.08 wt%. Our results showed that N-CDs/PMMA slab based luminescent solar concentrators make the practical use of LSCs more feasible because of the unique properties of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, environment pollution caused by oil, coal and so on have widely attracted attention, and it is urgent to develop new clean energy source [1,2]. Based on this, solar energy as a clean energy becomes popular [3]. Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) as alternatives to solar cells which typically consist of high refractive index waveguide materials and luminophores have gradually attracted interests since sunlight can be collected and re-emitted at a longer wavelength by luminophores and the emitted luminescence can be guided by waveguide towards PV devices attached at the slab edges, where it finally is able to be converted into electricity by the solar cell [4–7]. Therefore LSCs are suitable for photovoltaic windows if the waveguide matrix is transparent [8]. LSCs can achieve light concentration since the total LSC area exposed to sunlight is much larger than the surface area of the PV cells attached at the slab edges. So they can obviously cut the cost of expensive photovoltaic cells [9].

A variety of luminescent materials have been developed as emitters used for LSCs, among which semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) show obviously promising candidates due to their advantages [10–12]. To date, many kinds of semiconductor QDs have been used for LSC fabrication. For example, a series of core/shell semiconductor QDs such as CdSe/CdS [13], PbS/CdS [14], CdSe/CdPbS [15], and CdSe/CdZnS [16] QDs which exhibit a large Stokes shift have been synthesized and used for LSCs. Although semiconductor QDs have many unique properties including photostability, spectral tunability, and high photoluminescence quantum yield, they usually use heavy metals as elements and have high toxicity and cost, which significantly hinder their industrial applications [17–21]. Therefore, researchers still face the challenges how to fabricate LSCs with cost-effective approaches and environmentally friendly materials.

Very recently, new QDs with low toxicity such as inorganic perovskite, indium phosphide, CuInS2, and Si have been introduced which make them an ideal candidate for the emitters of LSCs [22–25]. However, these new QDs generally contain complex procedures of fabrications and high cost which definitely limit their practical use. Carbon-dots (CDs) [26], a type of green, low cost and low toxicity QDs discovered in 2004 have been widely used in biosensors, photocatalysis and bioimaging [27–33]. N-doping of CDs (N-CDs) exhibit unique properties such as high photoluminescence quantum yield, which means they might be efficient emitters of LSC devices. However, there is very few attempts on CDs for LSC fabrication in particular N-doped carbon-dots (N-CDs) [34]. The high photoluminescence quantum yield and photostability properties of N-CDs make them a very promising candidate for LSC fabrication [35].

In this paper, we used low cost and environmentally friendly N-doped carbon-dots (N-CDs) as efficient phosphors to fabricate N-CDs/PMMA slab based luminescent solar concentrators. The high-performance N-CDs/PMMA slab based luminescent solar concentrators with an optimal external optical efficiency of 12.23% could be achieved when the concentration of N-CDs within the PMMA matrix is 0.08 wt%. The usage of N-CDs in PMMA matrix as emitters makes the real applications of LSCs more feasible.

N-CDs, prepared by the one-step microwave method as reported by Li et al., [36] were used as the fluorescent material. Methyl methacrylate (MMA) (purity≥ 99.0%; supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China), polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) (purity≥ 99.0%; supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China), ethanol (C2H6O) (purity≥ 99.7%; supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) were used as received. 2, 2′- Azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, China) was used as initiator after being refined.

PMMA nanocomposite slabs were prepared by bulk polymerization of MMA in the presence of thermal radical initiator (AIBN). First, a blend of 20 mL of MMA monomer with 2.62 g of PMMA polymer was heated to 60 °C on a magnetic stirrer until the PMMA granules were dissolved completely. The blend was stirred vigorously for 1 h at 60 °C. 0.05 g of AIBN dissolved in 5 mL MMA monomer was added to the blend of MMA and PMMA. This blend solution was stirred and heated at 60 °C for 4 h. N-CDs (0.00 wt%, 0.02 wt%, 0.04 wt%, and 0.08 wt%) dispersed in 2 mL ethanol were added drop by drop to the blend of MMA and PMMA and the mixture was stirred for 10 min (60 °C) to form a homogeneous N-CDs/polymer slurry of appropriate viscosity, respectively. Then, the mixture was transferred into a casting mold. The casting mold was consisted of two glass plates with a silicone rubber between them, which were held firmly by steel clamps. Fig. 1 is a schematic illustration of the mixtures transferred into a casting mold. The casting molds filled with N-CDs/polymer slurry were placed in the oven at 50 °C for 16 h to complete curing. Finally, the N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs were cut into squares (20 mm × 20 mm × 2 mm) and polished around the edges, and then they were coupled with monocrystalline silicon solar cells (with an active area of 0.4 cm2) for subsequent testing. Fig. 2 is the photo of labeled N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs after post-processing (A0, A1, A2, and A3 refers to the slabs doped with 0.00 wt%, 0.02 wt%, 0.04 wt%, and 0.08 wt% N-CDs, respectively). As the concentration of N-CDs increases, the color of the N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs becomes darker.

UV–visible absorption spectra [37–39] were recorded with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV2600, Shimadzu Corp., Tokyo, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra [40] were measured at room temperature with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (QM/TM/NIR, Photon Technology International Corp., USA) at an excitation wavelength ranging from 340 nm to 530 nm and a scan speed of 600 nm/min. The photovoltaic properties [41] of quantum dot LSCs (QD-LSCs) with different N-CDs concentrations were obtained with a solar simulator (PVIV-4112 V, Newport Corp., USA) at an intensity of 100 mW/cm2 (1 sun). The active illuminated area of all QD-LSCs was 2.0 cm × 2.0 cm.

The absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra [42] of N-CDs in ethanol solutions are shown in Fig. 3 . It showed that N-CDs had a broad absorption band in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm, while PL emission spectra was centered at ∼435 nm, indicating very small overlap between absorption and emission spectra, namely N-CDs in ethanol solutions exhibit a large Stokes shift, which means N-CDs are a promising candidate for LSC fabrication. Absorption spectra of N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs with different concentrations of N-CDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. The intensity of absorption increased with the increase of N-CDs concentration, accompanied by the red shift (from 344 nm to 359 nm) of absorption peaks. It can be explained that with the increase of N-CDs concentration, more and more N-CDs absorb light so the absorption intensity is enhanced. And there may be slight agglomeration of N-CDs, which is reflected in the absorption spectra as a red shift of the absorption peak. Fig. 4b shows corresponding optical transmission spectra of Fig. 4a. It can be clearly seen that the visible light transmission of sample A0 (0 wt% N-CDs, used as control sample) is about 89%. Meanwhile, for sample A1, A2 and A3 were about 82%, 75% and 62%, respectively. Due to more and more N-CDs participating in the photon absorption process, the transmission decreases as the concentration of N-CDs increases. Furthermore, all the N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs show excellent visible light transmittance, which means our fabricated N-CDs/PMMA based LSCs can realize semi-transparent photovoltaic windows.

In order to further investigate the optical properties of the N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs, the emission spectra were assessed by using light at different wavelengths for excitation. As shown in Fig. 5 , the three samples (A1, A2, A3) exhibited an excitation-dependent PL behavior, which is common in fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). So far, the mechanisms of excitation-dependent PL behavior of CDs are not fully understood. Zhao et al. [43] attributed the behavior to the different size of CDs. Meanwhile, passivated surface defects of the core carbon particles may also lead to the excitation-dependent PL behavior of CDs. Furthermore, the maximum emission intensity can be obtained when excited at 370 nm.

Fig. 6 a and b show photoluminescence spectra of N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs with different concentrations of N-CDs under 370 nm and 470 nm excitation, respectively. The intensity of emission spectra increased with the increase of N-CDs concentration, and a slight red shift of the emission peak could be detected. That is to say, sample A3 has the maximum fluorescence intensity and the largest fluorescence emission peak position at the same excitation wavelength. The increasement of fluorescence intensity is mainly due to more N-CDs which absorb and emit photons. The red shift of the fluorescence emission peak can be attributed to the self-absorption effect of N-CDs. There is some overlap between the absorption spectra and the emission spectra, so partially overlapped light could be reabsorbed by N-CDs. The probability that the fluorescence emitted by one fluorescent particle encounters another fluorescent particle increases with the increase of N-CDs concentration, and the wavelength of the fluorescent light emitted after reabsorption also correspondingly undergoes a red shift, resulting in a slight red shift of the fluorescence emission peak.

Fig. 7 is a photograph of pure polymer slab (A0) and N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slab (A3) under UV excitation in dark (λ = 365 nm). As shown in Fig. 7, compared to the A0 sample, the A3 sample doped with 0.08 wt% N-CDs emitted bright light under ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 365 nm. The slab edges collect more light due to the total reflection of light in N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slab. In this case, if we couple a monocrystalline silicon solar cell at the edge of N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slab, the light intensity per unit area that collects on the solar cell will greatly increase, as well as the number of photoelectrons excited in the solar cell. Therefore, more effective photoelectric conversion can be achieved.

The electrical parameters of the LSCs were also measured with a solar simulator at an intensity of 100 mW/cm2 in this study. The results of these measurements are shown in Table 1 . Short-circuit current density (J
sc ), open-circuit voltage (V
oc ), fill factor (FF) and overall power conversion efficiency (η) are reported for the solar simulator. The optical efficiency (η
opt ) and η are respectively calculated with the following equations [44].
(1)
η
o p t
=
I
L S C
⋅
A
e d g e
I
S C
⋅
A
t o p
(2)
η =
I
S C
V
O C
F F
P ⋅
A
t o p
where I
LSC is the short-circuit current of the solar cell coupled to the LSC when the LSC is illuminated from the top, and I
sc is the short-circuit current measured from the same solar cell under direct illumination with the same light source; A
edge is the area of one side edge of the LSC; A
top is the area of the LSC top surface; P is the irradiation intensity.

The J–V characteristic curves of the LSCs with different N-CDs concentrations are shown in Fig. 8 . Results indicate that the photocurrent density obviously increases with the increase of N-CDs concentration at the same voltage.

The photovoltaic parameters such as V
oc , J
sc , FF, η
opt , and η are listed in Table 1. It can be seen that the power conversion efficiency increases with the increase of N-CDs concentration. As for pure PMMA based LSC (without N-CDs), the values of V
oc , J
sc , FF, η
opt , and η are 0.44 V, 5.75 mA/cm2, 58.86%, 7.58% and 1.48%, respectively. Meanwhile, the values of V
oc , J
sc , FF, η
opt , and η could reach up to 0.47 V, 9.29 mA/cm2, 60.72%, 12.23% and 2.63%, respectively, for the N-CDs/PMMA based LSC with 0.08 wt% concentration of N-CDs. Both J
sc and V
oc increase with the increase of N-CDs concentration. It is reported that J
sc is related to its transmission and the amount of photoelectrons excited in the solar cell [45], so J
sc has a great influence on the efficiency of the solar cell. The amount of excited photoelectrons is associated with the doping concentration of N-CDs. The intensity of the emitted fluorescence will increase if the concentration of N-CDs increases. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity on the monocrystalline silicon solar cell also increases, resulting in more photoelectrons being excited in the solar cell.

However, it is clear that the doping concentration of N-CDs cannot be increased indefinitely. The doping concentration of N-CDs has an optimal value. When the concentration of N-CDs exceeds this optimal value, the fluorescence intensity will not increase but decrease. This is because there is a certain overlap between the absorption spectra and the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, and the photons emitted from N-CDs may be absorbed by themselves. As the concentration of N-CDs increases, the probability of collision between them will also increase, which could exacerbate the self-absorption effect. Moreover, even if N-CDs can re-emit new photons after absorbing the emitted photons, since the fluorescence quantum efficiency must be smaller than 1, the final emitted fluorescence intensity is definitely smaller than the absorbed fluorescence intensity, resulting in a loss of total fluorescence intensity and the power conversion efficiency.

There are two reasons why we don't continue to increase the concentration of N-CDs. The first reason is that according to Fig. 8, the photocurrent density of sample A3 is just slightly bigger than that of sample A2. Compared with A1, the photocurrent density of sample A2 increases more. It shows that the increase of N-CDs concentration has less effect on the QD-LSC efficiency when the concentration of N-CDs reaches a certain value, so it is not necessary to continue increasing the concentration. Secondly, attempts have been made to increase the concentration of N-CDs during the experiment, such as increasing the concentration of N-CDs to 0.1 wt%, but macroscopic aggregation [46] of N-CDs could be clearly observed in N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slabs under the same preparation conditions. As a result, the optical waveguide doesn't appear uniform and transparent, which seriously affects its optical performance.

In conclusion, N-CDs were embedded in PMMA matrix for fabricate N-CDs/PMMA nanocomposite slab based LCSs. The optical properties and performance of N-CDs/PMMA slab based LSCs with different N-CDs concentrations were investigated. When the N-CDs/PMMA slab LSCs contained 0.08 wt% N-CDs, the values of V
oc , J
sc , FF, η
opt , and η could reach up to 0.47 V, 9.29 mA/cm2, 60.72%, 12.23% and 2.63%, respectively. The low cost and environmentally friendly N-doped carbon-dots (N-CDs) as efficient phosphors to fabricate high-performance N-CDs/PMMA slab based LSCs showed that N-CDs/PMMA slab based LSCs are a promising candidate for photovoltaic windows since they are transparent. In the future researches such as the efficiency vs. the size and the photostability of carbon dots etc. should be focused on for CDs based LSC fabrication.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21774098, 21104069), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities(WUT: 2017IVA089), and Open Foundation of the State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures at WUT (No. SYSJJ2018-03, and SYSJJ2018-04).
